editionStmt.edition: Digital Critical Edition of the Chapter Summaries (Capitula) to Leviticus in the Tours Bibles

publicationStmt.p:
Welcome to the digital critical edition project of the Capitula to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles!
The editions are part of the PRIN 2022 PNRR project DOBiPS – Data Oriented Biblical Paratext Studies , awarded for the 2023–2025 period to the research units at the Universities of Udine (P.I. E. Colombi) and Cassino (P.I. R. Casavecchia), under the competitive PRIN PNRR call – National Recovery and Resilience Plan, Mission 4 "Education and Research", funded by the European Union’s NextGenerationEU programme (protocol no. P2022ZW4AW).
The Capitula prefixed to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles have been encoded in XML/TEI and published using the software EVT (Edition Visualization Technology).
The editions were prepared by members of the Udine research unit: Emanuela Colombi, Margherita Filippozzi, and Silverio Franzoni.
Details of the encoding choices and related methodological reflections have been presented in the working paper: E. Colombi, M. Filippozzi, S. Franzoni, The Digital Critical Edition of the Capitula to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles , DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.16761173 .
Information on the individual editions is available by clicking the 'info' button at the top of the left-hand pane.

bibl.author: D. de Bruyne
bibl.title: Sommaires, divisions et rubriques de la Bible latine

bibl.author: Bonifatius Fischer
bibl.title: Lateinische Bibelhandschriften im frühen Mittelalter

bibl.author: Bernhard Bischoff
bibl.title: Katalog der festländischen Handschriften des neunten Jahrhunderts (mit Ausnahme der wisigotischen)

bibl.author: Ulysse Robert
bibl.title: Pentateuchi. Versio latina antiquissima e codice Lugdunensi. Version latine du Pentateuque antérieure a Saint Jérôme, publiée d'après le manuscrit de Lyon

bibl.author: Robertus Weber
Roger Gryson
bibl.title: Biblia sacra iuxta Vulgatam versionem

bibl.author: Ernst Ranke
bibl.title: Par palimpsestorum Wirceburgensium. Antiquissimae Veteris Testamenti versionis latinae fragmenta e codd. rescriptis

msDesc.head: Biblia latina, Pentateuchus. 1st volume of Maurdramnus' Bible

II + 351 folios

source.p: Before the Alcuin's Bibles according to Fischer 's classification (p. 256).

II + 214 folios
height 405 mm width 295 mm

source.p: Second period of the Turonian production according to Fischer 's classification (p. 259).

handDesc.handNote: Carolingian minuscule of ca. twelve hands

source.p: Second period of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 259).

handDesc.handNote: Carolingian minuscule of several different hands

source.p: Second period of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 260).
Second period of the Turonian production according to Fischer 's classification (p. 261).

origin.origPlace: Tours, Abbey St. Martin or Marmoutier ( Fischer , p. 263)

source.p: Fourth period of the Turonian production under Fridugisus (periode II) according to Fischer 's classification (p. 263).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti

source.p: Second period of the Turonian production under Adelard (periode III) according to Fischer 's classification (p. 264).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti (The Moutier-Grandval Bible)

source.p: Third period of the Turonian production according to Fischer 's classification (pp. 264-265).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina (First Bible of Charles the Bald)

I + 423 folios
height 495 mm width 375 mm

source.p: Fourth period (Abbot Vivian, 843-851) of the Turonian production according to Fischer 's classification (p. 267).

I + 382 + I folios
height 495 mm width 350 mm

source.p: Fifth period (853-c.900) of the Turonian production according to Fischer 's classification (p. 268).

III + 176 + III folios
height 490 mm width 365 mm

source.p: Fifth period (Abbot Vivian, 853-c.900) of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 269).

listWit.witness:
text of the A series as printed by D. de Bruyne , pp. 18-22.
digital facsimile

tagsDecl.rendition: the digital reproduction is b/w, impossible to determine color

handNotes.handNote: uncertainty whether in the scribe’s hand or added later

front.p:
This digital edition presents the reconstruction of the capitula of Series A (according to Donatien de Bruyne's classification) that precede the Book of Leviticus, in the textual form known from the Tours scriptorium.
To enhance the clarity of the critical apparatus, certain graphical and commentary devices have been adopted:
– In the reconstructed text, readings characteristic of the Turonian textual form are highlighted in comparison with those of the Maurdramnus' Bible (Amiens, Bibliothèques d'Amiens Métropole, Ms. 6, Corbie, 772-781 = Am1) and the Tours Octateuch (Tours, Bibliothèque municipale, Ms. 10, Tours, 790-800 = Tu).
Bold text indicates adiaphoric readings, i.e. readings equivalent to those attested in Am1 and/ or Tu.
Italicised text signals readings that are clearly erroneous, either exclusive to the Tours Bibles (to the entire corpus or at least to the majority of the Turonian witnesses) or shared with Am1 and/ or Tu; these readings have been included in the main text, as the aim is to reconstruct the archetypal form of the chapter headings from which the Tours Bibles descend.
Lemmas in the main text, which are highlighted in various colors depending on the cause of the variation, flag readings that differ, showing either a divergence between the Turonian corpus (= Φ) and one or both of the other branches (Am1, Tu), or a divergence from the reading chosen by D. de Bruyne in his edition of the biblical summaries.
- Additionally, in the left window displaying the reconstructed text, clickable red dots provide brief annotations indicating whether a reading is adiaphoric (adiaph.) or erroneous (error; excepting the cases of lectiones deteriores in orthography, which can hardly be considered proper errors), and whether it is attested only in the Tours Bibles (= Φ) or also in Am1 or Tu. The opposing witness is introduced with the abbreviation "vs".
Innovations of Am1 or Tu that are not shared with the Tours Bibles are not specifically marked, but can be identified through the apparatus displayed in the right-hand window.
– The Critical Apparatus includes notes, accessible via the "Critical note" tab when present, which comment where necessary on remarkable textual choices and the possible genesis of certain variants, with particular attention to those related to the Old Latin text of Leviticus, as attested in the Codex Lugdunensis (Lyon, Bibliothèque municipale, 403, 6th cent. = VL 100, edited by U. Robert, Paris, 1881), the Codex Wirceburgensis (Würzburg, Universitätsbibliothek, M.p.th.f.64a, 5th cent., palimpsest = VL 103, edited by E. Ranke, Wien, 1871) or, where these are lacunose, as conjectured on the basis of the Septuagint or of patristic quotations.
- Furthermore, brief annotations on the paleographic features of the witnesses are included as "witDetail" element, accessible via the XML tab in the Critical Apparatus, where they are deemed useful to clarify the codification.
- Finally, the "Analogues" tab - located near the "Critical Apparatus" tab in the top-right pane - lists the biblical passages to which each chapter heading refers based on the marginal numbering in the manuscripts - with the positioning of these numerals in Am1, Tu, and Sg1 serving as the primary reference. These passages are also accessible by clicking the small icon preceding each heading's numeral in the reconstructed text on the left. The biblical passages are quoted primarily from the Vulgate. Where necessary to understand the source of the summary, the Old Latin version(s) is also quoted. Additional commentary on the textual relationship between the headings and the biblical text, or on peculiar lexical choices, is provided where necessary and accessible via the "Bibliographic Reference" tab within the Analogues' apparatus.
These features allow users to quickly grasp the most salient textual phenomena and their philological implications.
This series of capitula appears to have been modelled on an Old Latin translation of Leviticus. The evidence for this comes from lexical choices that match the Old Latin version transmitted by the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) (e.g., ch. 21 puellam instead of the Vulgate's feminam; ch. 22 tactus instead of plaga) and the one of the Codex Wirceburgensis (VL 103) (e.g., ch. 53 uentriloquos et incantatoribus instead of the Vulgate's magos and ariolis), or that match the Septuagint where Old Latin witnesses are lacking (e.g., ch. 44 non ambulabis dolose : οὐ πορεύσῃ δόλῳ, a sentence not translated in the Vulgate; ch. 72 dona : τὰ δῶρα instead of the Vulgate's panes; ch. 87 bellum : πόλεμος instead of gladius, which is found not only in the Vulgate but also in the Codex Lugdunensis). Against this general agreement with a more literal, Greek-influenced translation, a few exceptions are noteworthy: ch. 2, 7, and 31, whose phrasing seems to echo the Vulgate quite exactly.
Regarding the textual transmission, it is possible, firstly, to conjecture a common archetype prior to the Tours Octateuch, the Maurdramnus' Bible, and the entire Turonian corpus. This archetype would be responsible for the corruptions that differently affect them in ch. 68 (patris uel in Φ versus patri uel in Tu and patruum in Am1; confratris in Φ and Am1 versus cum fratris in Tu). To this common exemplar should also be attributed the (almost) unanimously shared erroneous readings quae in ch. 16 and aequae in ch. 56.
A direct dependence of Φ on either Am1 or Tu can be ruled out, since each of the latter exhibits at least one innovation that would hardly have been restored, namely duobus coloribus (ch. 49) in Am1 and futurum... non (ch. 87) in Tu. On the whole, the textual character of Tu appears more altered than that of Am1, due to a greater number of exclusive lectiones deteriores or proper errors (even though easily emendable); Am1, by comparison, presents only 4 certain errors against the 13 found in Tu.
No closer relationship seems to exist between Φ and Am1, since the Maurdramnus' Bible shares only two innovations with all the Turonian witnesses: the inferior readings manducare (ch. 14) and decimo (ch. 28), which could easily have arisen independently in the two branches.
Conversely, the relationship between Φ and Tu appears slightly more relevant, as the Tours Octateuch and all the Turonian Bibles share two proper errors: concubito (ch. 65) and azyma (ch. 77). They also share two further lectiones deteriores: the omission of tuum in ch. 47 and concubito (ch. 63). However, these readings carry little stemmatic weight, as they are easily emendable by conjecture (indeed, the correct reading is found in a few Turonian witnesses, in addition to Am1; for the same reason, it cannot be ruled out that these errors/ innovations were already present in the common archetype).
The descent of Φ and Tu from a common exemplar - rather than Φ's direct dependence on Tu - is also prompted by the fact that the inferior reading concubito in ch. 63 was emended to coito in Tu - an attempted correction that was not adopted in Φ. However, a first-hand examination would be required to establish whether this modification in Tu was made by the main hand or by a later corrector.
The Turonian corpus is characterized by a small number of common unique innovations, which suggest that all the witnesses derive from a common exemplar. Particularly relevant are the adiaphoric reading per se in ch. 30 (versus sibi of Am1 and Tu), which was likely inspired by the Vulgate, and the various altered forms of prostituatur (ch. 71), whose origin seems to stem from an exemplar in which two variant readings were present. Further distinctions include servantur in ch. 88, versus the subjunctive found in Am1 and Tu (perhaps the better reading), and the erroneous quicumque in ch. 66 (the correct quaecumque in some Turonian witnesses may be a conjectural restoration). Since there are no innovations defining sub-groups within the Turonian corpus, it must be concluded that its witnesses are all collateral apographs, each derived independently from the common archetype.
Unique innovations among the Turonian Bibles are generally not significant, being limited to grammatical slips or banalizations. A sole exception is the oldest Turonian Bible, St. Gallen, Stiftsbibliothek, 75 (Sg1), in which a corrector intervened (ch. 50, 57, 71) apparently to conform the transmitted text to the Vulgate. Unfortunately, the Grosse Hartmut Bibel (St. Gallen, Stiftsbibliothek, 77) - likely an apograph of Sg1 - has a lacuna spanning from Ex 37:29 to Lv 5:17, making it impossible to verify whether these innovations were transmitted.

rdgGrp.witDetail: The scribe accidentally omitted the letter -i in incipiunt since no abbreviation mark appears above the word.

bibl.title: Lv 1:1 Vocauit autem mosen

item.title: Locutus loquutus est dominus ad moysen de tabernaculo testimonii et de sacrificiis

bibl.title: Lv 4:1 Locutusque est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 4:2 anima cum peccauerit per ignorantiam : VL 100 anima quaecumque deliquerit ante deum inprudenter. The phrasing of the heading seems to reflect the Vulgate text more than the Old Latin version, as attested in the Codex Lugdunensis. However, in both these versions, the verb ignorare is used later, at Lv 4:13 quod si omnis turba israhel (VL 100 si autem omnis synagoga filiorum israhel) ignorauerit.

item.title: Quid qu?d qui quid offeratur pro delicto ignorantiae ignorantiȩ ignorantiæ The reading ignorantiæ a appears to have been written over an erasure, but this cannot be confirmed without firsthand examination. a sacerdote sacerdotȩ uel populo populi dicit moysi moyses

bibl.title: Lv 5:1 Si peccauerit anima

item.title: Quid pro neglegentia neglegentiae negligentia

bibl.title: Lv 6:1 Locutus est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 6:3 siue rem perditam inuenerit et infitians insuper peierarit : VL 100 aut inuenerit perditum et mentitus fuerit pro eo et iurauerit iniuste.

item.title: De commendato conmendato et inuento si mentiatur aut iuret iu?ret iuret iniuste

bibl.title: Lv 6:8 Locutus est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 6:9 haec est lex holocausti. In Sg1, the erroneously omitted marginal numeral is supplied by a later hand in black ink.

item.title: De holocausto holocasto holocausto

bibl.title: Lv 6:20 Haec est oblatio aaron

item.title:
De sacerdote sacerd?te uncto et quid offerant offerunt The scribe of the Tours Octateuch wrote the abbreviated form offer̄, which most likely stands for offerunt. However, the subjunctive offerant, which is attested by the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Turonian corpus, is more apt for expressing a rule (see above, ch. 2 quid offeratur). The present indicative offerunt aligns with the Old Latin version transmitted by the Codex Lugdunensis (VL100) in the related passage: Lv 6:20 hoc munus [...] quod offerunt.
Φ Am1 (adiaph.) vs Tu

bibl.title: Lv 6:25 Loquere aaron et filiis eius
bibl.note: . The marginal numeral is here in the Tours Octateuch and in Cod. Sang. 75, immediately before in the Maurdramnus' Bible (Lv 6:24 Locutus est dominus ad mosen). The chapter heading appears to echo the Vulgate text of Lv 7:1 quite literally (lex hostiae pro delicto : VL 100 lex arietis eius qui est pro neclegentia), even though the term delictum is also employed in the Old Latin version of the Codex Lugdunensis in the preceding paragraphs.

item.title: Hostiam pro delicto

bibl.title: Lv 7:18 Si quis de carnibus uictimae
bibl.note: . The marginal numeral is here in the manuscripts, even though the chapter heading refers more precisely to Lv 7:23-26 adipem [...] non comedetis [...] et eius animalis quod a bestia captum est [...] sanguinem quoque omnis animalis non sumetis in cibo (VL 100 omnem adipem [...] non manducabitis [...] et a bestiis captorum [...] omnem sanguinem non manducabitis).

item.title:
Adipem et sanguinem et a fera comestum comestumm The scribe inadvertently wrote both the abbreviation mark indicating -m and the letter -m itself.
non manducandum

bibl.title: Lv 7:28 Locutus est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 7:29 qui offert victimam pacificorum : VL 100 qui offeret sacrificium salutaris.

item.title: De sacrificio sacrifitio salutari

bibl.title: Lv 8:1 Locutusque est dominus ad mosen

item.title:
Lauit la?uit lauit moyses The scribe left a blank space (ca. 2 letters) after moyses; it is impossible to determine whether a word was erased here without firsthand examination. aaron et filios eius ei?? et induit eum
eum uestem sacerdotalem

bibl.title: Lv 8:4 Fecit moses ut dominus imperarat
bibl.note: . The marginal numeral appears here in the manuscripts. However, this biblical passage seems to match the previous heading more closely (cfr. Lv 8:6-7 cumque lauisset eos uestiuit pontificem subucula linea : VL 100 et lauit eos aqua et induit eum tunicam). Nevertheless, this heading (ch. 11) in fact refers precisely to Lv 8:12 and following.

item.title:
Unxit uncxit uncxit unxit aaron et filios eius uestiuit
et obtulerunt optulerunt holocausta et sacrificia sacrifitia et descendit discendit ignis et consumpsit sumpsit consumpsit consumsit omnia

bibl.title: Lv 10:1 Arreptisque nadab et abiu filii aaron

item.title: Nadab nabad et abiud abiu abiud abiu abiud abiuth Abiud is the name of Aaron's son as attested in Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) at Lv 10:1, a form which matches the Greek Ἀβιουδ. The reading abiu, found in two of the Turonian Bibles as the primary text, corresponds to the Vulgate form of this Jewish name. offerentes ignem alienum igni igne gni igni igni igne The ablative igne is attested from the late Republican age onward, alongside the earlier form in -i (cf. ThLL, 288, s.v. ignis). caelesti cȩlesti consumpti sunt

bibl.title: Lv 10:8 Dixit quoque dominus ad aaron
bibl.note: The phrasing of this heading appears to align more closely with the Old Latin version, as attested in the Codex Lugdunensis, which follows the Greek (Lv 10:9 ἢ προσπορευομένων ὑμῶν πρὸς τὸ θυσιαστήριον : VL 100 aut introeuntibus uobis ad altare), than with the Vulgate's corresponding passage, where this sentence is omitted.

item.title:
Iubet
dominus This addition, which represents a unique innovation of P3 within the Turonian corpus, may have been suggested by the Vulgate text (Lv 10:8 dixit quoque dominus, which reflects the Septuagint's κύριος), while the Old Latin version in Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) reads et locutus est deus. sacerdotes sacerdos cum accedunt ad altare uinum non bibere

bibl.title: Lv 10:12 Locutusque est moses ad aaron
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 10:12 tollite sacrificium quod remansit [...] et comedite illud : VL 100 accipite sacrificium quod remansit [...] et manducate azyma. In this chapter heading, as in the subsequent ones, the use of manducare (or manducari) aligns with the Old Latin version of this passage, in contrast to the Vulgate, which employs forms of comedere or vesci.

item.title: De sacrificio quod remanserit remanseri?t remanserit remansit sacerdotes cum suis praecipit praecepit precipit praecepit praecipit The scribe appears to have added a variant reading rather than amending the main text, as there are no visible correction marks. praecepit praecipit praecepit manducare manducari Φ Am1 (adiaph.) vs Tu

bibl.title: Lv 11:1 Locutus est dominus ad mosen et aaron
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 11:2 haec sunt animalia quae comedere debetis de cunctis animantibus terrae : VL 100 haec pecora quae manducabitis ex pecoribus quae sunt super terram.

item.title: De pecoribus manducandis

bibl.title: Lv 11:9 Haec sunt quae gignuntur in aquis et uesci licitum est : VL 100 et haec manducabitis ex omnibus quae sunt in aquis

item.title:
De piscibus quae quae qua quae qui quȩ Unanimously attested by the Maurdramnus' Bible, the Tours Octateuch, and the Turonian Bibles, the relative pronoun quae likely reflects the Biblical text, where aquatic animals are denoted by a collective neuter phrase, both in the Old Latin Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) and in the Vulgate. The reading qui in P1 appears to be a conjectural improvement, intended to make the pronoun agree with the masculine piscibus.
manducentur

bibl.title: Lv 11:13 Haec sunt quae de auibus comedere non debetis : VL 100 haec quae abominamini ex uolatilibus et non manducabitur

item.title: De uolatilibus uolatibus uolatilibus non manducandis

bibl.title: Lv 11:20 Omne de uolucribus
bibl.note: . In Lv 11:20-40, various species of animals that are forbidden to be eaten are listed. This chapter heading subsumes the different species under the term pecus, which is used in the Old Latin version of the Codex Lugdunensis as the generic term for animals, while animal is used in the Vulgate (see above, ch. 15 and the note; cfr. also Lv 11:26 omne animal : VL 100 in omnis pecoribus).

item.title: De pecoribus pocoribus non manducandis mandis manducandis

bibl.title: Lv 11:41 Omne quod reptat super terram : VL 100 et omne quod [sic] repens quod repet super terram

item.title: De reptilibus reptibus reptilibus inmundis immundis

bibl.title: Lv 12:1 Locutus est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 12:2 Mulier si suscepto semine pepererit masculum.

item.title: De muliere mulierem muliere mulierem quae quæ qui quȩ masculum pepererit peperit pepererit peperit pepererit peperit peperit pepererit Probably corrected from an earlier reading, but this is not clearly detectable from the digital facsimile. peperit pepererit

bibl.title: Lv 12:5 Sin autem feminam pepererit : VL 100 si autem puellam pepererit

item.title:
De ea quae puellam pepererit peperit peperit pepererit peperit pepererit Probably corrected from an earlier reading, but this is not clearly detectable from the digital facsimile. Since this chapter heading closely resembles the preceding one, the subjunctive pepererit may be considered the archetypal reading. This is despite the fact that, conversely to ch. 20, the three traditional branches of the tradition are not unanimous. The alternative reading is attested by the Maurdramnus' Bible and, among other Turonian Bibles, by the oldest one, Cod. Sang. 75. Nevertheless, the subjunctive is syntactically preferable in a context of prescription and ruling and it reflects the phrasing found in both the Old Latin version of the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) and the Vulgate in the passages to which headings 20 and 21 refer.
Φ praeter Sg1 L2 Bm + Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1

bibl.title: Lv 13:1 Locutus est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 13:2 Homo in cuius carne et cute ortus fuerit diversus color siue pustula aut quasi lucens quippiam id est plaga leprae : VL 100 homini si cui facta erit in corio corporis eius tactus leprae.

item.title: De tactu tactum actu tactu leprae

bibl.title: Lv 14:1 Locutusque est dominus ad mosen

item.title:
Mundatum mutatum mandatum mundatum
a
lepra ostendi sacerdoti sacerdote et sic intrare in castra

bibl.title: Lv 14:33 Locutus est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 14:34 si fuerit plaga leprae in aedibus : VL 100 et dedero tactum leprae in domibus.

item.title: De macula leprae leprȩ in domo domu domo domu

bibl.title: Lv 15:1 Locutusque est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 15:2 uir qui patitur fluxum seminis : VL 100 uiro cuicumque factus fuerit fluxus de corpore eius.

item.title:
De uiro cui fuerit fluxus ex corpore
ex corpore

bibl.title: Lv 15:19 Mulier quae redeunte mense patitur fluxum sanguinis : VL 100 mulier quaecumque fuerit fluens sanguine et erit fluxus eius in corpore

item.title: De muliere quae quæ fuerit in fluxu sanguinis

bibl.title: Lv 16:1 Locutusque est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 16:2 ne omni tempore ingrediatur sanctuarium : VL 100 non introeat omni hora in sanctum interius.

item.title: Non n?? debere aaron ingredi assidue adsidue assiduae assiduȩ interius in sancta

bibl.title: Lv 16:29 Mense septimo decima die mensis adfligetis animas vestras : VL 100 in mensem septimum decima mensis humiliabitis animas uestras

item.title:
Septimo mense decimo decima The feminine decima in the Tours Octateuch appears to be more likely the archetypical reading, since it aligns with the Biblical text of both the Old Latin Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) and the Vulgate. Φ Am1 (error?) vs Tu die
di die mensis ieiunandum

bibl.title: Lv 17:1 Et locutus est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 17:5-6 ideo offerre debent sacerdoti filii israhel hostias suas [...] ut sanctificentur Domino ante ostium tabernaculi testimonii[...] fundetque sacerdos sanguinem : VL 100 ut offerant filii israhel sacrificia sua [...] et offerent domino ad ianuam tabernaculi testimonii et ad sacerdotem [...] et effundet sacerdos sanguinem.

item.title: Sacrificia per dei sacerdotes offerenda

bibl.title: Lv 17:10 Homo quilibet de domo israhel
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 17:12 omnis anima ex uobis non comedet sanguinem : VL 100 omnis anima ex uobis non manducabit sanguinem

item.title:
Sanguinem et per se sibi prae se per se The abbreviation mark in the amended reading p(er) se is not clearly legible due to faded ink. The reading sibi mortuum in the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Tours Octateuch aligns with the Old Latin version in the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100): Lv 17:15 sibi mortuum. Indeed, sibi mortu* is the usual translation for the Greek θνησιμαῖον adopted in the Codex Lugdunensis' version (e.g. Lv 11:8, 11:27, 11:24, 11:35). In contrast, the Vulgate adopts morticin* and cadaver*; an exception occurs at Lv 5:2, where θνησιμαίου is translated as per se mortuum - which is exactly the reading of the Turonian Bibles, even though this heading refers to Lv 17:15, where the Vulgate reads morticinum. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that the following chapter heading appears to refer to the same passage as ch. 30 (see below, the analogue passage of ch. 31). However, while ch. 30 reflects an Old Latin version - at least in two branches of the manuscript tradition, namely Am1 and Tu, ch. 31 follows closely the Vulgate text of Lv 17:15. It seems therefore likely that the latter was drawn from the Vulgate and inserted into the series to specify that the prohibition applies to animals that have died naturally (morticinum) as well as to those killed by other beasts (captum a bestia, which translates the Greek θηριάλωτον).
Φ (adiaph.) vs Am1 Tu mortuum non manducandum manducandu?

bibl.title: Lv 17:15 Anima quae comederit morticinum uel captum a bestia : VL 100 omnis anima quae manducauerit sibi mortuum aut uenationem captum
bibl.note: . See the critical note in the apparatus for the preceding chapter heading on the reading per se.

item.title: Morticinum et a fera fara færa captum non manducanda manducandum manducandum manducanda

bibl.title: Lv 18:1 Locutusque est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 18:6 omnis homo ad proximam sanguinis sui non accedet ut revelet turpitudinem eius : VL 100 homo homo ad omnia domestica carnis suae non accedet denudare.

item.title:
Cum omni The scribe left a blank space (ca. 2 letters) after omni; it is impossible to confirm whether a word was erased here without firsthand examination.
domestica generis non dormiendum

bibl.title: Lv 18:22 cum masculo non commisceberis coitu femineo : VL 100 et cum masculo non dormibis cubile muliebrae

item.title: Non adulterandum

bibl.title: Lv 19:1 Locutus est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 19:2-3 sancti estote quia ego sanctus sum dominus deus vester, unusquisque matrem et patrem suum timeat. The Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) has a lacuna from Lv 18:30 to 25:16.

item.title: Sanctos esse debere qui ad deum pertineant pertinent pertinent pertineant et parentes diligere dilegere

bibl.title: Lv 19:9 Cum messueris segetes terrae tuae
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 19:10 pauperibus et peregrinis carpenda dimittes. The Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) has a lacuna from Lv 18:30 to 25:16.

item.title:
Reliquias messis mensis mensis messis mensis messis Probably corrected from an earlier reading, but this is not clearly detectable from the digital facsimile. mensis messis
pauperibus pauperis dimittendas dimittendis demittendas

bibl.title: Lv 19:11 Non facietis furtum

item.title: Non furandum nec mentiendum nec ?ec periurandum neque delaturam faciendum faciendam faciend?? The abbreviation faciend̄ is ambiguous.

bibl.title: Lv 19:13 Non facies calumniam proximo tuo

item.title: Proximum non ledendum laedendum ledendum lȩdendum Probably corrected from an earlier reading, but this is not clearly detectable from the digital facsimile.

bibl.title: Lv 19:13 Non morabitur opus mercennarii

item.title: Mercedem mercennarii mer?cennarii mercennarii non detinendam detinendum detinendum detinendam retinendam

bibl.title: Lv 19:14 Non maledices surdo

item.title: Non maledices maladices mad maledices The scribe appears to have begun writing "mad[ices]" (or a similar error), but caught the mistake and corrected it. surdo surdos

bibl.title: Lv 19:14 Nec coram caeco pones offendiculum
bibl.note: . In Sg1, the marginal numeral was added by a corrector's hand in black ink.

item.title: Ante caecum cecum cecum cȩcum non pones offendiculum

bibl.title: Lv 19:14 Sed timebis deum tuum

item.title:
Dominum
deum tuum timebis

bibl.title: Lv 19:15 Non facies quod iniquum est
bibl.note: . In Sg1, the marginal numeral was added by a corrector's hand in black ink.

item.title: Non iudicabis iniuste

bibl.title: Lv 19:15 Nec consideres personam pauperis
bibl.note: . The chapter heading seems to echo a more literal translation (i. e. Old Latin), that in turn mirrors the Greek οὐ λήμψῃ πρόσωπον πτωχοῦ. The Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) has a lacuna at this point.

item.title: Personas non accipies accipias

bibl.title: Lv 19:16 Non eris criminator
bibl.note: . Like the previous one, this heading also appears to derive from a literal Old Latin translation that mirrors the Greek text: οὐ πορεύσῃ δόλῳ. The Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) has a lacuna at this point.

item.title: Non ambulabis dolose dolosȩ dolosae

bibl.title: Lv 19:16 Non stabis contra sanguinem proximi tui

app.witDetail: The scribe was writing an incorrect numeral but stopped to correct it.
The rubricated numeral V is less distinct due to faded ink.

item.title: Non insistes in sanguinem proximi promi proximi tui

bibl.title: Lv 19:17 Ne oderis fratrem tuum

item.title:
Non oderis fratrem tuum nec uindicabis iudicabis uindicabis te ipsum
te ipsum

bibl.title: Lv 19:18 Diliges amicum tuum sicut temet ipsum

app.note: The reading preserved in the Maurdramnus' Bible and in some witnesses within the Turonian corpus is most probably the archetypal one, as it matches the biblical text in both Greek (ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου) and the Vulgate (see the analogue). The mutilated state of the Codex Lugdunensis at this point precludes any comparison with the Old Latin version.

title.note: Φ praeter Z B2 L1 Bm + Tu (error) vs Am1

app.witDetail: Written with the abbreviated form tā-.

app.note: As it is apparently unattested elsewhere in the manuscript tradition, the variant of the Tours Octateuch - the addition of ipsum by a corrector's hand - is most likely a unique innovation originating from the influence of either the Vulgate (see the analogue) or the preceding chapter heading, which ends with this identical locution.

bibl.title: Lv 19:19 Iumenta tua non facies coire

item.title: Iumenta tua non sines coire diuerso generi genere

bibl.title: Lv 19:19 Veste quae ex duobus texta est non indueris

item.title:
Veste uestem uestes quae quæ quȩ quod ex
The preposition ex divides the Turonian corpus into two branches. The Maurdramnus' Bible aligns with the branch that includes it, while the Tours Octateuch and nearly half of the Turonian Bibles omit it. Even though the simplex ablative for expressing the quality of the clothing is grammatically correct, the prepositional construction may be considered the archetypal reading, since it aligns with both the Greek text (ἱμάτιον ἐκ δύο ὑφασμένον κίβδηλον) and the Vulgate (see the analogue passage). The mutilated state of the Codex Lugdunensis at this point precludes any comparison with the Old Latin version.
discoloribus duobus coloribus The use of discolor, usually an adjective, as a noun should be considered a lectio difficilior, which is attested elsewhere in the Vulgate: Ez 16:10 et vestivi te discoloribus ; cf. ThLL, 1336, s. v. (no comparison with the Old Latin version is possible here due to the lacunose condition of the Codex Lugdunensis). Conversely, the singular reading in the Maurdramnus' Bible may result from a banalization either of the likely archetypal reading ex discoloribus (where dis should be erroneously intended as an abbreviated form for duobus) or of the Vulgate text ex duobus, to which coloribus was added.
Φ Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1 est non
non indueris induaris indueris

bibl.title: Lv 19:20 Homo si dormierit cum muliere
bibl.note: . In Sg1, the marginal numeral was written by a corrector's hand in black ink.

app.witDetail: The scribe seems to have started writing an incorrect numeral but stopped to correct it.

item.title:
Si quis dormierit cum ancilla
disponsata desponsata disponsata desponsata disponsata desponsata uiro disponsata uiro
The expunction of disponsata uiro (uniformly transmitted by the manuscript tradition) in Cod. Sang. 75 may be interpreted as an attempt to align the chapter heading with the text of the Vulgate, where there is no exact correspondence for this phrase (Lv 19:20 cum muliere [...] quae sit ancilla etiam nubilis). The subpunctuation may also be intended to highlight a doubtful expression, rather than an actual deletion (for which, compare ch. 57, where principi is overstruck and replaced by idolo). However, disponsata uiro itself translates the Septuagint διαπεφυλαγμένη ἀνθρώπῳ. Since the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) has a lacuna at this point, one may instead consult the Old Latin version of Dt 22:23, where the concept of a woman promised or assigned to another man is also found: puella uirgo disponsata uiro (here the Greek term, however, is μεμνηστευμένη ἀνδρὶ) and Tertullian's De pudicitia, XX, which transmits a paraphrase of Lv 19:20: de famulae concubitu, quae homini esset reseruata.

bibl.title: Lv 19:26 Non augurabimini

item.title:
Non auguriandum augurandum augurandum auguriandum The form augurior appears to be attested for the classical auguror (s. ThLL, 1376, s.v.), and indeed auguriabimini is the form transmitted by the Maurdramnus' Bible, the Tours Octateuch and cod. St. Gal. 75 at this point in the Biblical text (see the analogue). For other occurrences of this verb in the Old Latin version transmitted by the Codex Lugdunensis (which has a lacuna at Lv 19:26), one may compare Gn 44:5 ipse enim augurio auguriatur in eo (but the participle augurans at Dt 18:10).
Φ praeter Sg1 a.c. Br1 B2 (adiaph.) vs Am1 Tu uel auspicandum a?spicandum aspicandum auspicandum

bibl.title: Lv 19:29 Ne prostituas filiam tuam

item.title: Non profanabis profanibi profanabis filiam tuam in moechiam moecham moecham moechiam

bibl.title: Lv 19:31 Ne declinetis ad magos
bibl.note: . The chapter heading appears to reflect a more literal translation (i. e. Old Latin), since the term uentriloquos is a loan translation of the Greek ἐγγαστριμύθοις, whereas in the Vulgate the latter is rendered as magos. Similarly, incantatores is a morphological calque of the Greek ἐπαοιδοῖς, a term the Vulgate instead translates as ariolis. Since the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) has a lacuna at this point, a comparison can instead be made with the Codex Wirceburgensis (VL 103): Non sequimini uentriloquos et incantatoribus non adhaerebitis.

item.title:
Ventriloquos uent?loquos uentriloquos uentriloquios uentriloquos uentriloquos uentiloquos
et incantatores cantatores incantatores non audiendos

bibl.title: Lv 19:32 Coram cano capite consurge
bibl.note: . Cfr. immediately after, Lv 19:32 honora personam senis : VL 103 honorabis faciem praesbyteri.

item.title:
Seniores et praesbyteros Written with the abbreviated form pr̄-. praesbiteros praesbyteros praesbiteros Written with the abbreviated form pr̄-. prȩsbiteros presbyteros
honorandos

bibl.title: Lv 19:33 Si habitauerit aduena
bibl.note: . In Sg1, the marginal numeral is added by a corrector's hand in black ink.

app.witDetail: The scribe was writing an incorrect numeral but stopped to correct it.

item.title: Aduenam adue?nam aduenam Probably corrected from an earlier reading, but this is not clearly detectable from the digital facsimile. diligendum

bibl.title: Lv 19:35 Nolite facere iniquum aliquid

app.witDetail: The scribe appears to have written the abbreviated form n̄suras; however, this is uncertain due to the poor quality of the digital facsimile.
The scribe appears to have accidentally skipped the letter -u, but noticed the error and corrected it.
app.note: The reading aequae, though grammatically unacceptable, must have stood in the archetypal form of this chapter heading, as it is attested almost unanimously (with slight orthographic divergences) across the three branches of the manuscript tradition. The better variant aeque was likely restored by conjecture in Tr and Bm within the Turonian corpus.

bibl.title: Lv 20:1 Locutusque est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 20:2 si quis dederit de semine suo idolo moloch morte moriatur : VL 103 quicumque dederit de semine suo principi moloch morti moriatur.

item.title:
Quicumque dederit filiam suam principi principi idolo The variant idolo, with which a corrector in Cod. Sang. 75 replaced the traditional reading, is inspired by the Vulgate (see the note in the apparatus of the analogue passage), whereas principi likely derives from an Old Latin text, as it is the literal translation of the Septuagint's ἄρχοντι. Since the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) has a lacuna at this point, a comparison can instead be made with the Codex Wirceburgensis (VL 103), which does indeed have principi.
moloch moloh moloc morte moriatur

bibl.title: Lv 20:6 Anima quae declinauerit ad magos
bibl.note: . See the critical note in the apparatus of the analogue passage at ch. 53. However, both Old Latin witnesses, the Codices Lugdunensis and Wirceburgensis, have a lacuna at this point.

app.witDetail: The rubricated numeral III is less distinct due to faded ink.

item.title: Quicumque secutus sequutus fuerit uentriloquos uentriloquos uentiloquos uentriloqos uentriloquos aut au aut ait aut praecantatores perire de dei populo

bibl.title: Lv 20:9 Qui maledixerit patri suo et matri

item.title: Qui maledixerit maledixeri patri aut uel aut uel matri moriatur morte moriatur morte moriatur moriatur The reading morte is underlined in black ink, though the exact significance of this marking is uncertain. While the text transmitted by the Tours Octateuch and almost the entire Turonian corpus is acceptable, the added reading morte matches the biblical text more exactly, both in the Septuagint (θανάτῳ θανατούσθω) and in the Vulgate (morte moriatur). No comparison is possible with the Old Latin version, as the Codices Lugdunensis (VL 100) and Wirceburgensis (VL 103) have a lacuna at this point. However, the fact that this addition is underlined in K (see the witDetail element, accessible via the XML tab) may indicate that the reading was not part of the archetypal form of this chapter heading. It may, indeed, originate from the interference of ch. 57, which ends exactly with this locution. Φ praeter K(?) + Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1

bibl.title: Lv 20:10 Si moechatus quis fuerit

app.witDetail: The rubricated numeral is faint due to faded ink.

item.title: Adulteri pariter moriantur moriatur

bibl.title: Lv 20:11 Qui dormierit cum nouerca sua
bibl.note: . The phrasing of this heading likely reflects an Old Latin literal translation of the Greek text (μετὰ γυναικὸς τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτοῦ), whereas the Vulgate uses nouerca. The Old Latin witnesses Lugdunensis (VL 100) and Wirceburgensis (VL 103) have a lacuna at this point.

app.witDetail: The rubricated numeral is faint due to faded ink.

item.title: Cum uxore oxore uxore patris qui dormierit ambo moriantur

bibl.title: Lv 20:12 Si quis dormierit cum nuru sua

item.title: Si quis manserit marit maanserit cum nuru nutru nuru sua ambo moriantur moria?ntur Without a firsthand examination it is impossible to determine whether there was an erased letter in the blank space (ca. 1 letter) after moria-.

bibl.title: Lv 20:13 Qui dormierit cum masculo

item.title:
Quicumque manserit cum masculo concubito concubitu cubito coito concubitu concubito The variant coito in the Tours Octateuch is likely a conjectural attempt - inspired by the Vulgate version of Lv 20:13 (coitu femineo) - to correct the corrupted reading in the main text, which must originally have matched that of the Turonian corpus (see also below, ch. 65). The reading attested in the Maurdramnus' Bible and in three Bibles within the Turonian corpus is superior, as it aligns with classical Latin declension.
Φ praeter P8 Bm Mb2 a.c. vs Am1 vs Tu muliebri ambo moriantur

bibl.title: Lv 20:14 Qui supra uxorem filiam duxerit

item.title: Quicumque acceperit mulierem et matrem eius ei?? The abbreviation mark is unclear, because the ink is faded. igni cremabuntur

bibl.title: Lv 20:15 Qui cum iumento et pecore

app.witDetail: The scribe was writing an incorrect numeral but stopped to correct it.

item.title: Quicumque dederit concubito concubitum concubitu concubitu concubito Φ praeter P8 Mb2 a.c. + Tu (error) vs Am1 in quadrupedem moriatur moriantur et quadrupedem occidetis

bibl.title: Lv 20:16 Mulier quae subcubuerit

item.title: Quicumque quecumque quaecumque qu?cumque quacumque quicumque quȩcumque quȩcumque Φ praeter Sg1 P4 Br1 p.c. P3 (error) vs Am1 vs Tu mulier ad aliquod aliquos pecus pedes accesserit causa coitus cotus coitus occidantur

bibl.title: Lv 20:17 Qui acceperit sororem suam

item.title:
Quicumque acceperit sororem suam The scribe left a blank space (ca. 5 letters) after suam; it is impossible to determine whether a word was erased here.
extirpabuntur extirpabunt?? The presence of the abbreviation mark for -ur is uncertain due to faded ink.
de populo

bibl.title: Lv 20:18 Qui coierit cum muliere in fluxu

item.title:
Si quis cum cam cum menstruata menstrua menstruata coierit uel et cum uxore uxorem patris patruum patri The three branches of the manuscript tradition appear to stem from a common corrupted exemplar. The chapter heading refers to Lv 20:20, echoing closely, at least apparently, the Vulgate text: qui coierit cum uxore patrui uel auunculi sui, which explicates more clearly the generic μετὰ τῆς συγγενοῦς αὐτοῦ of the Septuagint; no comparison with the Old Latin version is possible, since the Codices Lugdunensis (VL 100) and Wirceburgensis have a lacuna at this point. Therefore, the reading of the Turonian Bibles and that of the Tours Octateuch may result from a banalization of patrui, while patruum in the Maurdramnus' Bible - with the omission of the following uel - may be a misreading of patrui (ve)l. However, a similar passage at Lv 18:14 can be compared, where the Codex Lugdunensis has confusionem fratris patris tui (mirroring the Greek ἀσχημοσύνην ἀδελφοῦ τοῦ πατρός σου closely), instead of turpitudinem patrui tui of the Vulgate; thus, it cannot be ruled out that the pre-archetypal form of this heading had this same expression (cum uxore fratris patris), preserved incompletely in the Turonian Bibles.
Φ (error) vs Am1 vs Tu uel
auunculi uel confratris cum fratris confratri As in the preceding case, the common exemplar was corrupted at this point, and its reading was likely that preserved by the Tours Octateuch. However, the text as transmitted in Tu - in which uxore is implicit - is syntactically quite harsh (the biblical reference is Lv 20:21 qui duxerit uxorem fratris sui in the Vulgate). Consequently, either uxore has been lost in the tradition, or the preposition cum must be expunged (the latter being a more economical solution).
Φ praeter Mb2 Bm P1 + Am1 (error) vs Tu sui

bibl.title: Lv 20:27 Vir siue mulier in quibus pythonicus
bibl.note: The heading, with the loan translations uentriloquum and incantatorem (ἐγγαστρίμυθος ἢ ἐπαοιδός in the Greek text), which also occur in ch. 53 and 58 (see above), likely derives from an Old Latin version (the Codex Lugdunensis has a lacuna at this point; in the Codex Wirceburgensis the terms are translated as in utero loquens et incantator). In the Vulgate, however, where forms of magus and ariolus are used in those chapters, here the expression in quibus pythonicus uel diuinationis fuerit spiritus is employed instead.

item.title:
Ventriloquum uentrilocum uentriloquum uentrilocum uentriloquum uentiloquum
et incantatorem incantatores cantatorem incantatorem lapidandos
lapitandos lapitandos lapidandos

bibl.title: Lv 21:1 Dixit quoque dominus ad mosen [...] ne contaminetur sacerdos : VL 103 et locutus est dominus ad moysen [...] non coinquinabuntur
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 21:8 sit ergo sanctus.

item.title: Sacerdotes in nullo inquinari sed esse sanctos

bibl.title: Lv 21:9 Sacerdotis filia si deprehensa
bibl.note: . In Sg1, he marginal numeral is added by a corrector's hand in black ink.

item.title:
Filia uiri uiri
The expunction of uiri in Cod. Sang. 75 was in all probability due to a corrector who intended to match the chapter heading with the Vulgate text, whereas it seems to reflect an Old Latin version in its original form; the Codices Lugdunensis and Wirceburgensis have a lacuna at this point, but the Septuagint may be compared: θυγάτηρ ἀνθρώπου ἱερέως.
sacerdotis si prostituatur prostitutur prostituatur prostia prostituatur The scribe seems to have started writing prostia[tur] (or something similar) by mistake, but stopped to correct it. prostitoatur prostituatur prostituator prostitoatur prostituatur prostitoatur prostitoatur prostituatur It seems that both variant readings prostitu- and prostito- were in some way present in the common exemplar of the Tours Bibles, perhaps prostitu- in the main text, with an -o added in the interlinear space. Thus, the variant in Tr may be explained as a false emendation, resulting from a misinterpretation of the interlinear addition and a misplacement of the -o. ad fornicandum igni igne igne igni The scribe appears to have added a variant reading rather than amending the main text, as there are no visible correction marks. cremabitur cremabuntur

bibl.title: Lv 21:16 Locutusque est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 21:17 homo [...] qui habuerit maculam non offeret panes deo suo. The heading, with non accedet offerre dona, appears to derive from an Old Latin text rather than the Vulgate (non offeret panes). Since the Codices Lugdunensis and Wirceburgensis have a lacuna at this point, the Septuagint version can be used for comparison: Ἄνθρωπος [...] οὐ προσελεύσεται προσφέρειν τὰ δῶρα τοῦ θεοῦ αὐτοῦ.

item.title: Homo in quo fuerit macula non accedet acce?det accedet offerre dona dona offerre deo dei

bibl.title: Lv 22:1 Locutus quoque est dominus
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 22:3 omnis homo qui accesserit [...] ad ea quae consecrata sunt [...] in quo est inmunditia peribit coram domino; Lv 22:10 omnis alienigena non comedet de sanctificatis.

item.title:
Sacerdotem in quo fuerit inmunditia inmundicia inmundititia inmundicia inmunditia immunditia et sic si accesserit adcesserit ad sancta exterminari a deo et de sanctis extraneus ec extraneus The scribe appears to have started writing ec[straneus], but stopped to amend it.
non manducet man?ducet Compare the reading as written in Am1, with a blank space (ca. 1 letter) between man- and -ducet.

bibl.title: Lv 22:17 Locutus est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 22:20 si maculam habuerit non offeretis; 22:25 de manu alienigenae non offeretis [...] quia corrupta et maculata sunt omnia.

item.title: Maculosa et uitiosa non offerenda

bibl.title: Lv 22:29 Si immolaueritis hostiam pro gratiarum actione
bibl.note: . Since the Codices Lugdunensis (VL 100) and Wirceburgensis (VL 103) have a lacuna at this point, the Septuagint version can be used for comparison: ἐὰν δὲ θύσῃς θυσίαν εὐχὴν χαρμοσύνης.

item.title:
De
de sacrificio sacrifitio orationis

bibl.title: Lv 23:1 Locutus est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 23:3 dies septimus quia sabbati requies est vocabitur sanctus.

item.title: De sabbato obseruando obseruando sabbato sabbato obseruando

bibl.title: Lv 23:4 Hae sunt ergo feriae domini sanctae
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 23:5-6 [...] phase (VL 103 pascha) domini est et [...] sollemnitas azymorum domini est.

app.witDetail: The rubricated numeral is faint due to faded ink.

item.title:
De pascha et paschae pascha et paschae pascha et p?scha et pascha et azyma azymis azima azima azyma
Φ Tu (error) vs Am1

bibl.title: Lv 23:9 Locutusque est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 23:10 feretis [...] primitias messis uestrae ad sacerdotem.

app.witDetail: The rubricated numeral is faint due to faded ink.

item.title:
De primitiis primiciis primi?iis messis mensis mensis messis mensis messis
offerendis

bibl.title: Lv 23:23 Locutusque est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 23:24 mense septimo prima die mensis erit uobis sabbatum.

app.witDetail: The rubricated numeral is faint due to faded ink.

item.title: De mense septimo

bibl.title: Lv 24:1 Et locutus est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 24:2 ut adferant tibi oleum [...] ad concinnandas lucernas.

item.title: Ad lumen lucernae lucernȩ lucerne oleum inferendum

bibl.title: Lv 24:10 Ecce autem egressus filius
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 24:16 qui blasphemaerit [...] lapidibus opprimet eum omnis multitudo.

item.title: Eum qui blasphemauerit lapidandum lapitandum lapidandum

bibl.title: Lv 24:17 Qui percusserit et occiderit hominem
bibl.note: . Cfr. 25:10 sanctificabisque annum quinquagesimum [...] ipse est enim iobeleus.

item.title:
Eum eeum The scribe wrote eum, after which the rubricator added the initial letter in rubrication.
qui occiderit occider?? The verb's ending -it is nearly illegible; without a first-hand examination it is impossible to determine whether the faint ink results from an intentional deletion or from accidental fading.
occidendum et de iubeleo iubileum iubileo iubeleo

bibl.title: Lv 25:25 Si adtenuatus frater tuus : VL 100 si autem pauper erit frater tuus

item.title: De d? fratre paupere pauperi pauper?

bibl.title: Lv 25:39 Si paupertate conpulsus vendiderit se

item.title: De fratre uenundato et comparato conparato The scribe wrote the abbreviated form cō-.

bibl.title: Lv 26:1 Non facietis uobis idolum et sculptile : VL 100 non facietis uobis idola manufacta
bibl.note: . The marginal numeral is found here in Am1 and Tu, but on the preceding line in Sg1 (Lv 26:1 Ego dominus deus uester).

app.witDetail: The scribe was writing an incorrect numeral but stopped to correct it.

item.title: Idola non n?? facienda

bibl.title: Lv 26:1 Ego enim sum dominus deus
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 26:2 custodite sabbata mea.

app.witDetail: The rubricated numeral is faint due to faded ink.

item.title: Sabbata seruanda

bibl.title: Lv 26:2-3 Ego dominus si in praeceptis meis
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 26:3 si [...] mandata mea custodieritis.

item.title:
Bellum afuturum futurum afuturum non adfuturum futurum afuturum affuturum The actual phrasing of this heading appears to align more closely with the Septuagint's text: Lv 26:6 πόλεμος οὐ διελεύσεται, than with either the Old Latin version of the Codex Lugdunensis: gladius non transiet (in the Codex Wirceburgensis, a lacuna extends from Lv 23:9 to the end of this book) or the Vulgate: gladius non transibit. The participle afuturum is problematic within the manuscript tradition, perhaps because it lacks a direct equivalent in the biblical text. In the Tours Octateuch, its corrupted form futurum may have prompted the conjectural emendation non custodiantur, which preserves the original meaning (it must be noted, however, that the entire chapter is written by the main hand with no visible corrections). In contrast, within the Turonian corpus, this same reading has been subject to unnecessary modifications - in textual layers later than the main text - such as those found in the manuscripts Sang. 75 or Paris. lat. 1. However, in the former, the original meaning is preserved by the addition of a negative particle before the participle (non adfuturum).
Φ praeter P8 Sg1 p.c. P1 p.c. + Am1 (adiaph.) vs Tu si mandata m??data manda mandata custodiantur non custodiantur Φ Am1 (adiaph.) vs Tu

bibl.title: Lv 26:13 Ego dominus deus uester qui eduxi
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 26:15 ut non faciatis ea quae a me constituta sunt : VL 100 ut uos non faciatis omnia mandata mea.

item.title: In tribulationibus et fame et est et The earlier reading was likely the abbreviated form ē for est. plagis malis malis?? malis Probably corrected from an earlier reading, but this is not clearly detectable from the digital facsimile. males castigandos castigandis si praecepta precepta præcepta prȩcepta pracepta non seruantur seruentur Even though the reading of the Turonian Bibles cannot be classified as a proper error, the subjunctive form transmitted by the Tours Octateuch and the Maurdramnus' Bible is grammatically preferable and is indeed likely the archetypal reading. Φ (error?) vs Am1 Tu

bibl.title: Lv 27:1 Locutusque est dominus ad mosen
bibl.note: . Cfr. Lv 27:2 homo qui uotum fecerit et spoponderit deo animam suam : VL 100 quicumque orauerit orationem pretium animae illius domino; Lv 27:9-10 animal autem quod immolari potest domino [...] mutari non poterit : VL 100 si autem a pecoribus offerentibus munus domino [...] non mutabis illud.

item.title:
Votum masculi mascul? uel The scribe left a blank space (ca. 4 letters) after uel; it is impossible to determine whether a word was erased here.
feminae femine feminȩ praetio pretio praecio precio praetio pretio Probably corrected from an earlier reading, but this is not clearly detectable from the digital facsimile.
offerendum in pecudibus uero non mutandum
